jsdoc.documentation: A simpple JSDoc comment.

jsdoc.insertText:
/**
 * ${1:What it does}.
 *
 * @param ${3:name} - ${4:Parameter description.}
 * @returns ${2:Type and description of the returned object.}
 *
 * @example
 * ```
 * ${5:Write me later.}$0
 * ```
 */
jsdoc.documentation: A full JSDoc comment with description, parameters, return, and example.

jsdoc.label: @arg
jsdoc.documentation: Provides the name, type, and description of a function parameter. Synonysm of @param and @argument.

jsdoc.label: @see
jsdoc.documentation: Refer to some other documentation for more information. It accepts text or {@link foobar}.

jsdoc.label: @var
jsdoc.documentation: Document a member. Synonysm or @member.

jsdoc.label: @desc
jsdoc.documentation: The @description tag allows you to provide a general description of the symbol you are documenting. The description may include HTML markup. It may also include Markdown formatting if the Markdown plugin is enabled. Synonysm of @description.

jsdoc.label: @enum
jsdoc.documentation: The @enum tag documents a collection of static properties whose values are all of the same type.

jsdoc.label: @file
jsdoc.documentation: The @file tag provides a description for a file. Use the tag in a JSDoc comment at the beginning of the file. Synonysm of @fileoverview and @overview.

jsdoc.label: @func
jsdoc.documentation: This marks an object as being a function, even though it may not appear to be one to the parser. Synonysm of @function and @method.

jsdoc.label: @host
jsdoc.documentation: The @external tag identifies a class, namespace, or module that is defined outside of the current package. Synonysm of @external.

jsdoc.label: @kind
jsdoc.documentation: What kind of symbol is this. Possible values are: class, constant, event, external, file, function, member, mixin, module, namespace, typedef.

jsdoc.label: @link
jsdoc.documentation: Link to another item in the documentation. Synonysm of @linkcode and @linkplain.

jsdoc.label: @name
jsdoc.documentation: The @name tag forces JSDoc to associate the remainder of the JSDoc comment with the given name, ignoring all surrounding code. This tag is best used in "virtual comments" for symbols that are not readily visible in the code, such as methods that are generated at runtime.

jsdoc.label: @this
jsdoc.documentation: The @this tag indicates what the this keyword refers to when used within another symbol.

jsdoc.label: @todo
jsdoc.documentation: Documents a task to be completed.

jsdoc.label: @type
jsdoc.documentation: Documents the type of the object.

jsdoc.label: @alias
jsdoc.documentation: Treat a member as if it had a different name.

jsdoc.label: @async
jsdoc.documentation: Indicate that the function is asynchronous.

jsdoc.label: @class
jsdoc.documentation: This function is intended to be called with the "new" keyword. Synonysm of @constructor.

jsdoc.label: @emits
jsdoc.documentation: Describe the events this method may fire. Synonysm of @fires.

jsdoc.label: @event
jsdoc.documentation: Provide an example of how to use a documented item. The text that follows this tag will be displayed as highlighted code.

jsdoc.label: @fires
jsdoc.documentation: Describe the events this method may fire. Synonysm of @emits.

jsdoc.label: @inner
jsdoc.documentation: Using the @inner tag will mark a symbol as an inner member of its parent symbol. This means it can be referred to by "Parent~Child".

jsdoc.label: @lends
jsdoc.documentation: The @lends tag allows you to document all the members of an object literal as if they were members of a symbol with the given name. You might want to do this if you are passing an object literal into a function that creates a named class from its members.

jsdoc.label: @mixes
jsdoc.documentation: This object mixes in all the members from another object.

jsdoc.label: @mixin
jsdoc.documentation: Document a mixin object.

jsdoc.label: @param
jsdoc.documentation: Provides the name, type, and description of a function parameter. Synonysm of @arg and @argument.

jsdoc.label: @since
jsdoc.documentation: Version in which this symbol was added.

jsdoc.label: @access
jsdoc.documentation: Specify the access level of this member (private, package-private, public, or protected).

jsdoc.label: @author
jsdoc.documentation: Identify the author of an item.

jsdoc.label: @global
jsdoc.documentation: Document a global object.

jsdoc.label: @ignore
jsdoc.documentation: Omit a symbol from the documentation.

jsdoc.label: @member
jsdoc.documentation: Document a member. Synonysm or @var.

jsdoc.label: @method
jsdoc.documentation: This marks an object as being a function, even though it may not appear to be one to the parser. Synonysm of @function and @func.

jsdoc.label: @module
jsdoc.documentation: Document a JavaScript module.

jsdoc.label: @return
jsdoc.documentation: Documents the value a function returns. Synonysm of @returns.

jsdoc.label: @static
jsdoc.documentation: Indicates this is an static member.

jsdoc.label: @throws
jsdoc.documentation: Describe what errors can be thrown. Synonysm of @exception.

jsdoc.label: @yields
jsdoc.documentation: Document the value yielded by a generator function.

jsdoc.label: @borrows
jsdoc.documentation: The @borrows tag allows you to add documentation for another symbol to your documentation. This tag would be useful if you had more than one way to reference a function, but you didn't want to duplicate the same documentation in two places.

jsdoc.label: @default
jsdoc.documentation: The @default tag allows you to document the assigned value of a symbol. You can supply this tag with a value yourself or you can allow JSDoc to automatically document the value from the source code -- only possible when the documented symbol is being assigned a single, simple value that is either: a string, a number, a boolean or null. Synonysm of @defaultvalue.

jsdoc.label: @example
jsdoc.documentation: The @event tag allows you to document an event that can be fired. A typical event is represented by an object with a defined set of properties.

jsdoc.label: @exports
jsdoc.documentation: Use the @exports tag when documenting JavaScript modules that export anything other than the "exports" object or the "module.exports" property.

jsdoc.label: @extends
jsdoc.documentation: The @augments or @extends tag indicates that a symbol inherits from, and potentially adds to, a parent symbol. You can use this tag to document both class-based and prototype-based inheritance. Synonysm of @augments.

jsdoc.label: @license
jsdoc.documentation: Identify the license that applies to this code.

jsdoc.label: @listens
jsdoc.documentation: List the events that a symbol listens for.

jsdoc.label: @package
jsdoc.documentation: The @package tag marks a symbol as package-private. Typically, this tag indicates that a symbol is available only to code in the same directory as the source file for this symbol.

jsdoc.label: @private
jsdoc.documentation: Indicate this symbol is meant to be private.

jsdoc.label: @publilc
jsdoc.documentation: Indicated this symbol is meant to be public.

jsdoc.label: @returns
jsdoc.documentation: Documents the value a function returns. Synonysm of @return.

jsdoc.label: @summary
jsdoc.documentation: A shorter version of the full description.

jsdoc.label: @typedef
jsdoc.documentation: Documents a custom type definition.

jsdoc.label: @version
jsdoc.documentation: Documents the version number of an item.

jsdoc.label: @virtual
jsdoc.documentation: This member must be implemented (or overridden) by the inheritor. Synonysm of @abstract.

jsdoc.label: @abstract
jsdoc.documentation: This member must be implemented (or overridden) by the inheritor. Synonysm of @virtual.

jsdoc.label: @argument
jsdoc.documentation: Provides the name, type, and description of a function parameter. Synonysm of @param and @arg.

jsdoc.label: @augments
jsdoc.documentation: The @augments or @extends tag indicates that a symbol inherits from, and potentially adds to, a parent symbol. You can use this tag to document both class-based and prototype-based inheritance. Synonysm of @extends.

jsdoc.label: @callback
jsdoc.documentation: The @callback tag provides information about a callback function that can be passed to other functions, including the callback's parameters and return value. You can include any of the tags that you can provide for a @method.

jsdoc.label: @constant
jsdoc.documentation: Document an object as a constant. Synonysm of @const.

jsdoc.label: @external
jsdoc.documentation: The @external tag identifies a class, namespace, or module that is defined outside of the current package. Synonysm of @host.

jsdoc.label: @function
jsdoc.documentation: This marks an object as being a function, even though it may not appear to be one to the parser. Synonysm of @func and @method.

jsdoc.label: @instance
jsdoc.documentation: Using the @instance tag will mark a symbol as an instance member of its parent symbol. This means it can be referred to by "Parent#Child".

jsdoc.label: @linkcode
jsdoc.documentation: Link to another item in the documentation. Synonysm of @link and @linkplain.

jsdoc.label: @memberof
jsdoc.documentation: This symbol belongs to a parent symbol.

jsdoc.label: @override
jsdoc.documentation: The @override tag indicates that a symbol overrides a symbol with the same name in a parent class.

jsdoc.label: @overview
jsdoc.documentation: The @file tag provides a description for a file. Use the tag in a JSDoc comment at the beginning of the file. Synonysm of @file and @fileoverview

jsdoc.label: @property
jsdoc.documentation: The @property tag is a way to easily document a list of static properties of a class, namespace or other object.

jsdoc.label: @readonly
jsdoc.documentation: Indicates this symbol is meant to be read-only.

jsdoc.label: @requires
jsdoc.documentation: Indicates a required module.

jsdoc.label: @tutorial
jsdoc.documentation: Link to a tutorial file.

jsdoc.label: @classdesc
jsdoc.documentation: Use the following text to describe the entire class.

jsdoc.label: @copyright
jsdoc.documentation: Document some copyright information.

jsdoc.label: @exception
jsdoc.documentation: Describe what errors can be thrown. Synonysm of @throws.

jsdoc.label: @generator
jsdoc.documentation: Indicate that a function is a generator function.

jsdoc.label: @interface
jsdoc.documentation: This symbol is an interface that others can implement.

jsdoc.label: @linkplain
jsdoc.documentation: Link to another item in the documentation. Synonysm of @link and @linkcode.

jsdoc.label: @namespace
jsdoc.documentation: The @namespace tag indicates that an object creates a namespace for its members. You can also write a virtual JSDoc comment that defines a namespace used by your code.

jsdoc.label: @protected
jsdoc.documentation: The @protected tag marks a symbol as protected. Typically, this tag indicates that a symbol is only available, or should only be used, within the current module.

jsdoc.label: @variation
jsdoc.documentation: Distinguish different objects with the same name.

jsdoc.label: @constructs
jsdoc.documentation: This function member will be the constructor for the previous class.

jsdoc.label: @deprecated
jsdoc.documentation: Document that this is no longer the preferred way.

jsdoc.label: @implements
jsdoc.documentation: This symbol implements an interface.

jsdoc.label: @inheritdoc
jsdoc.documentation: Indicate that a symbol should inherit its parent's documentation.

jsdoc.label: @constructor
jsdoc.documentation: This function is intended to be called with the "new" keyword. Synonysm of @class.

jsdoc.label: @description
jsdoc.documentation: The @description tag allows you to provide a general description of the symbol you are documenting. The description may include HTML markup. It may also include Markdown formatting if the Markdown plugin is enabled. Synonysm of @desc.

jsdoc.label: @defaultvalue
jsdoc.documentation: The @default tag allows you to document the assigned value of a symbol. You can supply this tag with a value yourself or you can allow JSDoc to automatically document the value from the source code -- only possible when the documented symbol is being assigned a single, simple value that is either: a string, a number, a boolean or null. Synonysm of @default.

jsdoc.label: @fileoverview
jsdoc.documentation: The @file tag provides a description for a file. Use the tag in a JSDoc comment at the beginning of the file. Synonysm of @file and @overview.

jsdoc.label: @hideconstructor
jsdoc.documentation: Indicate that the constructor should not be displayed.

tsdoc.documentation: A simple TSDoc comment.
A full TSDoc comment with description, parameters, return, and example.

tsdoc.label: @see
tsdoc.documentation: The @see tag can be used to create a list of references for other resources related to this export.

tsdoc.label: @beta
tsdoc.documentation: This tag can be used to indicate that the associated member is intended to eventually be used by third-party developers but is not yet stable enough to conform to semantic versioning requirements.

tsdoc.label: @enum
tsdoc.documentation: If present on an object with string or number literal values, TypeDoc will convert the variable as an enumeration instead of a variable.

tsdoc.label: @link
tsdoc.documentation: The @link tag is used to refer to another documented declaration. The tag has the form of {@link myDeclaration}.

tsdoc.label: @alpha
tsdoc.documentation: This tag can be used to indicate that the associated member is intended to eventually be used by third-party developers but is not yet stable enough to conform to semantic versioning requirements.

tsdoc.label: @event
tsdoc.documentation: The @event tag is used to mark a reflection as belonging in the "Events" group. It is equivalent to specifying @group Events in the comment.

tsdoc.label: @group
tsdoc.documentation: The @group tag can be used to place several related API items under a common header when listed in a page's index. It may be specified multiple times to list a reflection under several headings.

tsdoc.label: @label
tsdoc.documentation: The @label tag can be used to give an overloaded signature a name that it can be referenced with via a declaration reference.

tsdoc.label: @param
tsdoc.documentation: The @param tag is used to document a parameter of a function or method.

tsdoc.label: @ignore
tsdoc.documentation: Reflections marked with the @hidden tag will be removed from the documentation. It is equivalent to the @ignore JSDoc tag.

tsdoc.label: @module
tsdoc.documentation: The @module tag is used to mark a comment as referring to a file rather than the declaration following it. It may optionally be used to rename a module whose name TypeDoc guesses incorrectly.

tsdoc.label: @public
tsdoc.documentation: This tag should generally not be used. The @public tag overrides the visibility of a reflection to be public. This does not strictly conform to the TSDoc specification, which treats member visibility and release visibility separately.

tsdoc.label: @sealed
tsdoc.documentation: TypeDoc parses the @sealed tag for compatibility with TSDoc, but does not attach any meaning to it's use.

tsdoc.label: @throws
tsdoc.documentation: The @throws tag can be used to document an exception that can be thrown by a function or method.

tsdoc.label: @example
tsdoc.documentation: The example tag indicates that the following text is an example of how to use the function.

tsdoc.label: @private
tsdoc.documentation: This tag should generally not be used and may be removed in a future release. The @private tag overrides the visibility of a reflection to be private.

tsdoc.label: @remarks
tsdoc.documentation: The @remarks tag can be used to separate the summary section of a documentation content from additional detailed information. At most one @remarks block is permitted in a comment. Unlike most tags, the @remarks tag will be copied if {@inheritDoc} is used in a comment.

tsdoc.label: @returns
tsdoc.documentation: The @returns tag can be used to document the return value of a function. At most one @returns tag should be present in a comment.

tsdoc.label: @virtual
tsdoc.documentation: TypeDoc parses the @virtual tag for compatibility with TSDoc, but does not attach any meaning to it's use.

tsdoc.label: @category
tsdoc.documentation: The @category tag can be used to place several related API items under a common header when listed in a page's index. It may be specified multiple times to list a reflection under several headings.

tsdoc.label: @internal
tsdoc.documentation: The @internal tag indicates that a reflection is not intended to be used by API consumers. API items annotated with @internal may be removed from the generated documentation by specifying the --excludeInternal option.

tsdoc.label: @overload
tsdoc.documentation: The @overload tag is recognized for use in JavaScript projects which can use it to declare overloads since TypeScript 5.0. It is automatically removed from the rendered documentation with the --excludeTags option.

tsdoc.label: @override
tsdoc.documentation: TypeDoc parses the @override tag for compatibility with TSDoc, but does not attach any meaning to it's use.

tsdoc.label: @property
tsdoc.documentation: The @property (or @prop) tag can be used to add a comment to a child of the current reflection. It is intended for use with the @namespace and @interface tags which might not have convenient places to include comments for each member.

tsdoc.label: @readonly
tsdoc.documentation: The @readonly tag indicates that a reflection should be documented as non-writable, even if writable according to TypeScript.

tsdoc.label: @template
tsdoc.documentation: The @template tag is used to document a type parameter of a function, method, class, interface or type alias. It is used like "@template {string} T - the identity type "

tsdoc.label: @interface
tsdoc.documentation: If present on a type alias, will cause it to be converted as an interface. This will result in all "dynamic" properties being expanded to real properties.

tsdoc.label: @protected
tsdoc.documentation: This tag should generally not be used and may be removed in a future release. The @protected tag overrides the visibility of a reflection to be protected.

tsdoc.label: @satisfies
tsdoc.documentation: This tag is recognized for parity with TypeScript 5.0's @satisfies Support in JSDoc.

tsdoc.label: @typeParam
tsdoc.documentation: The @typeParam tag is used to document a type parameter of a function, method, class, interface or type alias. TypeDoc recognizes the @template tag as an alias of @typeParam.

tsdoc.label: @deprecated
tsdoc.documentation: nThe @deprecated tag indicates that a declaration should not be used and may be removed in a future release.

tsdoc.label: @inheritDoc
tsdoc.documentation: The @inheritDoc tag is used to create a reflection's documentation by copying it from another reflection. The tag has the form {@inheritDoc ref} where ref is a declaration reference.

tsdoc.label: @defaultValue
tsdoc.documentation: The @defaultValue tag can be used to document the default value for an accessor or property.

tsdoc.label: @experimental
tsdoc.documentation: This tag can be used to indicate that the associated member is intended to eventually be used by third-party developers but is not yet stable enough to conform to semantic versioning requirements.

tsdoc.label: @eventProperty
tsdoc.documentation: The @eventProperty tag is used to mark a reflection as belonging in the "Events" group. It is equivalent to specifying @group Events in the comment.

tsdoc.label: @privateRemarks
tsdoc.documentation: The @privateRemarks tag can be used to include documentation text that should not be included in the generated API reference.

tsdoc.label: @packageDocumentation
tsdoc.documentation: The @packageDocumentation tag is used to mark a comment as referring to a file rather than the declaration following it. The TypeDoc specific @module tag can be used for the same purpose when semantically clearer.

typescript.label: cc
cp
typescript.documentation: Creates and assigns a local variable using props destructing

typescript.label: cs
typescript.documentation: Creates and assigns a local variable using state destructing

typescript.label: api
cas
typescript.documentation: If the specified expression is false, the message is written to the console along with a stack trace

typescript.label: cco
typescript.documentation: Writes the the number of times that count() has been invoked at the same line and with the same label

typescript.label: cdi
typescript.documentation: Prints a JavaScript representation of the specified object

typescript.label: cdm
typescript.documentation: Invoked once, only on the client (not on the server), immediately after the initial rendering occurs.

typescript.label: cdm
typescript.documentation: Invoked once, only on the client (not on the server), immediately after the initial rendering occurs.

typescript.label: cer
typescript.documentation: Displays a message in the console and also includes a stack trace from where the method was called

typescript.label: cgd
typescript.documentation: Invoked after a component is instantiated as well as when it receives new props. It should return an object to update state, or null to indicate that the new props do not require any state updates.

typescript.label: cgr
typescript.documentation: Groups and indents all following output by an additional level, until console.groupEnd() is called.

typescript.label: cin
typescript.documentation: Displays a message in the console but also displays a blue information icon along with the logged message

typescript.label: clg
typescript.documentation: Displays a message in the console

typescript.label: clj
typescript.documentation: Logs stringified JSON property with name.

typescript.label: con
typescript.documentation: Adds a default constructor for the class that contains props as arguments

typescript.label: ctr
typescript.documentation: Prints a stack trace from the point where the method was called

typescript.label: cwa
typescript.documentation: Displays a message in the console but also displays a yellow warning icon along with the logged message

typescript.label: cwm
typescript.documentation: Invoked once, both on the client and server, immediately before the initial rendering occurs

typescript.label: cwr
typescript.documentation: Invoked when a component is receiving new props. This method is not called for the initial render.

typescript.label: dar
typescript.documentation: Creates and assigns a local variable using array destructing

typescript.label: dob
typescript.documentation: Creates and assigns a local variable using object destructing

typescript.label: est
typescript.documentation: Creates empty state object. To be used in a constructor.

typescript.label: fin
typescript.documentation: Iterating over property values of iterable objects

typescript.label: fof

terraform.label: tf-aws
tf-vcd
terraform.insertText:
provider "vcd" {
   user                 = "\${var.vcd_user}"
   password             = "\${var.vcd_pass}"
   org                  = "\${var.vcd_org}"
   url                  = "\${var.vcd_url}"
   vdc                  = "\${var.vcd_vdc}"
   maxRetryTimeout      = "\${var.vcd_maxRetryTimeout}"
   allow_unverified_ssl = "\${var.vcd_allow_unverified_ssl}"
}

terraform.label: tf-ibm_lb
tf-ibm_lb
terraform.insertText:
resource "ibm_network_gateway" "${MyResource}" {
 name = "test"
 ssh_key_ids = ["${data.ibm_compute_ssh_key.key.id}"]
 members {
 hostname             = "mygateway"
 domain               = "ibm.com"
 datacenter           = ""
 network_speed        = 
 private_network_only = false
 ssh_key_ids          = ["${data.ibm_compute_ssh_key.key.id}"]
 tcp_monitoring       = true
 process_key_name     = ""
 os_key_name          = ""
 redundant_network    = false
 disk_key_names       = [""]
 public_bandwidth     = 
 memory               = 
 tags                 = ["gateway tags 1", "terraform test tags 1"]
 notes                = "gateway notes 1"
 ipv6_enabled         = true
 }
}
terraform.documentation: define a ibm network gateway resource.

terraform.label: tf-module

terraform.label: tf-output

terraform.label: tf-triton
terraform.insertText:

   key_material = "\${file("~/.ssh/id_rsa")}"
   key_id       = "25:d4:a9:fe:ef:e6:c0:bf:b4:4b:4b:d4:a8:8f:01:0f"
   # If using a private installation of Triton, specify the URL
   url = "https://us-west-1.api.joyentcloud.com"
}

terraform.label: tf-aws_region
terraform.documentation: aws_region provides details about a specific AWS region. As well as validating a given region name (and optionally obtaining its endpoint) this resource can be used to discover the name of the region configured within the provider. The latter can be useful in a child module which is inheriting an AWS provider configuration from its parent module.

terraform.label: tf-aws_subnet

   data = "127.0.0.1"
    domain_id = "${ibm_dns_domain.myResourceName.id}"
    expire = 900
   minimum_ttl = 90

   retry = 1
   type = "a"
}

}
resource "ibm_lb_service_group" "${myResourceName}" {
   port =

The latter may be useful e.g. for adding network ACL rules.

tf-datadog_monitor
terraform.insertText:
resource "datadog_monitor" "${myResourceName}" {
   name = "my-${myResourceName}"
   description = "my-description-${myResourceName}"
   type = "metric alert"
   message = "Monitor triggered. Notify: @hipchat-channel"
   escalation_message = "Escalation message @pagerduty"

   query = "avg(last_1h):avg:aws.ec2.cpu{environment:foo,host:foo} by {host} > 2"

   thresholds {
       ok = 0
       warning = 1
       critical = 2
   }

   notify_no_data = false
   renotify_interval = 60

   notify_audit = false
   timeout_h = 60
   include_tags = true

   silenced {
       "*" = 0
   }
}

pydoc.label: ###test
pydoc.documentation:
Module-level docstrings for test files are not required. They should be included only when there is additional information that can be provided.

Examples include some specifics on how the test should be run, an explanation of an unusual setup pattern, dependency on the external environment, and so on. Docstrings that do not provide any new information should not be used, such as """Tests for foo.bar."""

pydoc.label: ###generator

cobol.label: identification
cobol.insertText:
identification division.
       program-id. ${1:${TM_FILENAME/(.*)\..+$/$1/}}.

       environment division.
       input-output section.
        file-control.
        select ${2:cust} assign to '${4:$2}.txt'
        organization is line sequential.            

       data division.
       file section.
       fd $2.
       01 ${3:$2-file}.
        03 ${5:customer-id}    pic 9(5).
        03 ${6:customer-info}  pic x(65).

       working-storage section.
       01 ws-eof       pic a. 

       procedure division.
          open input $2.
             perform until ws-eof='Y'
                read $2
                   at end
                      move 'Y' to ws-eof
                   not at end
                      display ${5} " => " ${6}
                end-read
             end-perform
          close $2.
          $0
          goback.

       end program $1.

cobol.label: IDENTIFICATION
cobol.insertText:
IDENTIFICATION DIVISION. 
        PROGRAM-ID. $1.
        DATA DIVISION.
        WORKING-STORAGE SECTION. 
        01 OUT-MSG.
         02 FILLER PIC X(6) VALUE "Hello ". 
         02 MSG    PIC X(20).
        01 INP-MSG PIC X(20) VALUE "What is your name? ".     
        PROCEDURE DIVISION.
        PARA1.
            EXEC CICS
                 SEND FROM(INP-MSG)
                 ERASE
            END-EXEC

            EXEC CICS
                 RECEIVE INTO (MSG)
            END-EXEC

       *
            EXEC CICS
                 SEND FROM (OUT-MSG)
                 ERASE
            END-EXEC

            EXEC CICS
                 RETURN
            END-EXEC.

       *
        END PROGRAM $1.

cobol.label: IDENTIFICATION
cobol.insertText:
IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. ${1:${1:${TM_FILENAME/(.*)\..+$/$1/}}}.
       AUTHOR. $2.
       INSTALLATION.  ${3:where}.
       DATE-WRITTEN.  ${4:$CURRENT_DATE/$CURRENT_MONTH/$CURRENT_YEAR}.
       DATE-COMPILED. ${5:$4}.
       SECURITY.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SOURCE-COMPUTER. ${6:pc}.
       OBJECT-COMPUTER. ${7:${6}}.
       SPECIAL-NAMES.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
       DATA DIVISION.
        FILE SECTION.
        WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
        LINKAGE SECTION.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
        DECLARATIVES.
        END DECLARATIVES.
        main-processing SECTION.
         mainline-paragraph.
             $0
             STOP RUN.

cobol.label: mfu,program-id
cobol.insertText:
program-id. MFUT_${1:${TM_FILENAME_BASE/MFUT[_](.*)/$1/}}.
       working-storage section.
       copy "mfunit.cpy".

       01 test-fail-count     binary-long value 0.

       *> Testcase     : $1
       *>  Description : $2
       procedure division.
           $0
           if 1 not equal 1
               display "Odd... 1 not equal 1?"
               add 1 to test-fail-count
           end-if

           if test-fail-count not equal 0
               call "MFU_ASSERT_FAIL_Z" using
                    by reference z"$1 has failed"
               end-call
           end-if

           goback returning test-fail-count.

       *> Startup for testcase $1
       entry "MFUS_$1".
           *> Open any files
           goback.

       *> Teardown for testcase $1
       entry "MFUE_$1".
           *> Close any files/delete any temp files
           goback.

       *> Metadata for testcase $1
       entry "MFUM_$1".
           move "$2" to MFU-MD-TESTCASE-DESCRIPTION
           goback.
       end program.

kdoc.documentation: A simple Kotlin comment block with short summary and description. Useful when the user prefers to manually add the other documentation tags.
A Kotlin comment block including short summary, description, param, return, and example.

kdoc.label: @see
kdoc.documentation: References another item or piece of documentation.

kdoc.label: @param
kdoc.documentation: Documents a value parameter of a function or a type parameter of a class, property or function. To better separate the parameter name from the description, if you prefer, you can enclose the name of the parameter in brackets. This is a synonym of @parameter.

kdoc.label: @since
kdoc.documentation: Version of Java when the the product was implemented.

kdoc.label: @author
kdoc.documentation: You can provide one @author tag, multiple @author tags, or no @author tags. For classes and interfaces only, required.

kdoc.label: @sample
kdoc.documentation: Embeds the body of the function with the specified qualified name into the documentation for the current element, in order to show an example of how the element could be used.

kdoc.label: @throws
kdoc.documentation: Documents an exception which can be thrown by a method. Since Kotlin does not have checked exceptions, there is also no expectation that all possible exceptions are documented, but you can still use this tag when it provides useful information for users of the class. Synonym of @exception.

kdoc.label: Example
kdoc.documentation: While there is no dedicated tag for examples on Kotlin, we can still include them as plain text. This allows you illustrate the usage or behavior of a particular code element.

kdoc.label: @example
kdoc.documentation: Adds an example to the documentation.

kdoc.label: @property
kdoc.documentation: Documents the property of a class which has the specified name. This tag can be used for documenting properties declared in the primary constructor, where putting a doc comment directly before the property definition would be awkward.

kdoc.label: @receiver
kdoc.documentation: Documents the receiver of an extension function.

kdoc.label: @suppress
kdoc.documentation: Excludes the element from the generated documentation. Can be used for elements which are not part of the official API of a module but still have to be visible externally.

kdoc.label: @exception
kdoc.documentation: Documents an exception which can be thrown by a method. Since Kotlin does not have checked exceptions, there is also no expectation that all possible exceptions are documented, but you can still use this tag when it provides useful information for users of the class. Synonym of @throws.

kdoc.label: @parameter
kdoc.documentation: Documents a value parameter of a function or a type parameter of a class, property or function. To better separate the parameter name from the description, if you prefer, you can enclose the name of the parameter in brackets. This is a synonym of @param.

kdoc.label: @Deprecated
kdoc.documentation: The @deprecated description in the first sentence should at least tell the user when the API was deprecated and what to use as a replacement. Only the first sentence will appear in the summary section and index. Subsequent sentences can also explain why it has been deprecated.

kdoc.label: @constructor
kdoc.documentation: Documents the primary constructor of a class.

mint.label: h
mint.documentation: HTML elements can be written as in standard HTML.

mint.label: p
mint.documentation: HTML elements can be written as in standard HTML.

mint.label: do
mint.documentation: do expressions are for two things: handling asynchronous computations that might fail, for example when loading something with a request, or executing asynchronous expressions sequentially.

mint.label: if
mint.documentation: The if...else conditional expression can return two different values based on a condition.

mint.label: att
mint.documentation: Attributes are either strings or expressions.

mint.label: com
mint.documentation: Components are reusable pieces of code that have specific behavior, styles and content.

mint.label: con
mint.documentation: The connect directive lets you connect a component to a store which allows you to call the stores functions and properties without using the stores name.

mint.label: div
mint.documentation: HTML elements can be written as in standard HTML.

mint.label: exp
mint.documentation: HTML Expressions allows inserting data into HTML elements or components.

mint.label: fun
mint.documentation: Functions can be defined on modules, components, stores and providers.

mint.label: get
mint.documentation: Computed properties works like regular properties but instead of returning a constant value it can return different values base on the state and the properties.

mint.label: mod
mint.documentation: Modules are kind of containers for a set of relatable functions, usually used to gather functions that relate to a specific type.

mint.label: sty
mint.documentation: Styles define with CSS how an HTML element looks.

mint.label: tag
mint.documentation: Tags that have the name of a component will render that component at the point where the tag is defined.

mint.label: try
mint.documentation: try is a control expression for handling synchronous computations that might fail, for example when you are trying to convert an untyped JavaScript object into a typed Record.

mint.label: case
mint.documentation: A case expression is useful for matching enums or exact values, while also supporting a default value.

mint.label: main
mint.documentation: The component named Main is the one that get's rendered on the screen.

mint.label: next
mint.documentation: Functions can be defined on modules, components, stores and providers.

mint.label: prop
mint.documentation: The property keyword when used in a store defines part of the data that the store contains.

mint.label: span
mint.documentation: HTML elements can be written as in standard HTML.

mint.label: event
mint.documentation: All event handlers are functions, they take an event record and return Void.

mint.label: store
mint.documentation: Stores are global containers of application specific data.

mint.label: button
mint.documentation: HTML elements can be written as in standard HTML.

mint.label: record
mint.documentation: Records are object like data structures, that have a fix set of keys and values.

mint.label: render
mint.documentation: The render function renders the component into an HTML tree.

mint.label: routes
mint.documentation: In Mint routes of an application are defined at the top level with the routes block.

yaml.label: k-job
yaml.insertText:
# https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/job/
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: ${1:myjob}
  namespace: ${2:default}
  labels:
    app: ${1:myjob}
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      name: ${1:myjob}
      labels:
        app: ${1:myjob}
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: ${1:myjob}
        image: ${3:python:3.7.6-alpine3.10}
        command: ['sh', '-c', '${4:python3 manage.py makemigrations && python3 manage.py migrate}']
        env:
        - name: ENV_NAME
          value: ENV_VALUE
        volumeMounts:
        - name: localtime
          mountPath: /etc/localtime
      volumes:
      - name: localtime
        hostPath:
          path: /usr/share/zoneinfo/Asia/Taipei
      restartPolicy: OnFailure
      dnsPolicy: ClusterFirst
---
$0

yaml.label: k-pod
yaml.insertText:
# https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/pods/
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: "${1:myapp}"
  namespace: ${2:default}
  labels:
    annotations:
      kubectl.kubernetes.io/default-container:  ${1:myapp}
    app: "${1:myapp}"
spec:
  containers:
  - name: ${1:myapp}
    image: "${3:debian-slim:latest}"
    resources:
      limits:
        cpu: 200m
        memory: 500Mi
      requests:
        cpu: 100m
        memory: 200Mi
    env:
    - name: DB_HOST
      valueFrom:
        configMapKeyRef:
          name: myapp
          key: DB_HOST
    ports:
    - containerPort: ${4:80}
      name: http
    volumeMounts:
    - name: localtime
      mountPath: /etc/localtime
  volumes:
    - name: localtime
      hostPath:
        path: /usr/share/zoneinfo/Asia/Taipei
  restartPolicy: Always
---
$0

yaml.label: k-pvc
yaml.insertText:
# https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/persistent-volumes/
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: ${1:myapp}
  namespace: ${2:default}
  labels:
    app: ${1:myapp}
spec:
  # AKS: default,managed-premium
  # GKE: standard
  # EKS: gp2 (custom)
  # Rook: rook-ceph-block,rook-ceph-fs
  storageClassName: ${3|default,managed-premium,standard,gp2,rook-ceph-block,rook-ceph-fs|}
  accessModes:
  - ${4|ReadWriteOnce,ReadWriteMany,ReadOnlyMany|}
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: ${5:2Gi}
---
$0

yaml.label: k-cronjob
yaml.insertText:
# https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/cron-jobs/
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: ${1:cronjobname}
  namespace: ${2:default}
spec:
  schedule: "${3:*/1 * * * *}"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
          - name: ${4:jobname}
            image: ${5:busybox}
            args: ['/bin/sh', '-c', '${6:date; echo Hello from the Kubernetes cluster}']
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
---
$0

yaml.label: k-ingress
k-service
k-configmap
k-daemonset
yaml.insertText:
# https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/daemonset/
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: ${1:myapp}
  namespace: ${2:default}
  labels:
    app: ${1:myapp}
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ${1:myapp}
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        kubectl.kubernetes.io/default-container:  ${1:myapp}
      labels:
        app: ${1:myapp}
    spec:
      tolerations:
      # this toleration is to have the daemonset runnable on master nodes
      # remove it if your masters can't run pods
      - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/master
        effect: NoSchedule
      containers:
      - name: ${1:myapp}
        image: ${3:debian}
        resources:
          limits:
            memory: 200Mi
          requests:
            cpu: 100m
            memory: 200Mi
        volumeMounts:
        - name: localtime
          mountPath: /etc/localtime
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30
      volumes:
      - name: localtime
        hostPath:
          path: /usr/share/zoneinfo/Asia/Taipei
---
$0

yaml.label: k-deployment
yaml.insertText:
# https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/deployment/
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: ${1:myjob}
  namespace: ${2:default}
  labels:
    app: ${1:myjob}
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ${1:myjob}
  replicas: 1
  strategy:
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 25%
      maxUnavailable: 25%
    type: RollingUpdate
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        kubectl.kubernetes.io/default-container:  ${1:myjob}
      labels:
        app: ${1:myjob}
    spec:
      # initContainers:
        # Init containers are exactly like regular containers, except:
          # - Init containers always run to completion.
          # - Each init container must complete successfully before the next one starts.
      containers:
      - name: ${1:myjob}
        image: ${3:myjob:latest}
        imagePullPolicy: ${4|IfNotPresent,Always,Never|}
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 100m
            memory: 100Mi
          limits:
            cpu: 100m
            memory: 100Mi
        livenessProbe:
          tcpSocket:
            port: ${5:80}
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          timeoutSeconds: 5
          successThreshold: 1
          failureThreshold: 3
          periodSeconds: 10
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /_status/healthz
            port: ${5:80}
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          timeoutSeconds: 2
          successThreshold: 1
          failureThreshold: 3
          periodSeconds: 10
        env:
        - name: DB_HOST
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: ${1:myjob}
              key: DB_HOST
        ports:
        - containerPort: ${5:80}
          name: ${1:myjob}
        volumeMounts:
        - name: localtime
          mountPath: /etc/localtime
      volumes:
        - name: localtime
          hostPath:
            path: /usr/share/zoneinfo/Asia/Taipei
      restartPolicy: Always
---
$0

yaml.label: k-ingress-tls
yaml.insertText:
# https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/ingress/#tls
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: ${1:testsecret-tls}
  namespace: ${2:default}
type: kubernetes.io/tls
# The TLS secret must contain keys named 'tls.crt' and 'tls.key' that contain the certificate and private key to use for TLS.
data:
  tls.crt: base64 encoded cert
  tls.key: base64 encoded key

---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: ${3:tls-example-ingress}
  namespace: ${2:default}
spec:
  tls:
  - hosts:
      - ${4:https-example.foo.com}
    secretName: ${1:testsecret-tls}
  rules:
  - host: ${4:https-example.foo.com}
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /${5}
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: ${6:service1}
            port:
              number: ${7:80}
---
$0

yaml.label: k-statefulset
yaml.insertText:
# https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/statefulset/
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: ${1:myapp}
  namespace: ${2:default}
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ${1:myapp} # has to match .spec.template.metadata.labels
  serviceName: "${1:myapp}"
  replicas: ${3:3} # by default is 1
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        kubectl.kubernetes.io/default-container:  ${1:myapp}
      labels:
        app: ${1:myapp} # has to match .spec.selector.matchLabels
    spec:
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 10
      containers:
      - name: ${1:myapp}
        image: ${4:${1:myapp}-slim:1.16.1}
        ports:
        - containerPort: ${5:80}
          name: ${1:myapp}
        volumeMounts:
        - name: ${6:www}
          mountPath: /usr/share/nginx/html
  volumeClaimTemplates:
  - metadata:
      name: ${6:www}
    spec:
      storageClassName: ${7:my-storage-class}
      accessModes:
      - ${8|ReadWriteOnce,ReadWriteMany,ReadOnlyMany|}
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: ${9:1Gi}
---
$0

yaml.label: k-ingress-rewrite
yaml.insertText:
# https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/ingress/
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: ${1:example-ingress}
  namespace: ${2:default}
  # https://kubernetes.github.io/ingress-nginx/examples/rewrite/
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /\$1
spec:
  rules:
  - host: ${3:example.foo.com}
    http:
      paths:
      - path: ${4:/api/(.*)}
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: ${5:service1}
            port:
              number: ${6:80}
---
$0

unity.documentation: Reset is called when the user hits the Reset button in the Inspector's context menu or when adding the component the first time.

unity.label: Start()
unity.documentation: Start is called on the frame when a script is enabled just before any of the Update methods is called the first time.

OnCollisionEnter is called when this collider/rigidbody has begun
 touching another rigidbody/collider.

tcl.label: cd
tcl.documentation: Change the current working directory to dirName, or to the home directory (as specified in the HOME environment variable) if dirName is not given. Returns an empty string. Note that the current working directory is a per-process resource; the cd command changes the working directory for all interpreters and (in a threaded environment) all threads.

tcl.label: if
tcl.documentation: if statement

tcl.label: for
tcl.documentation: For is a looping command, similar in structure to the C for statement. It returns an empty string.

tcl.label: ife
tcl.documentation: if else statement

tcl.label: set
tcl.documentation: Returns the value of variable varName. If value is specified, then set the value of varName to value, creating a new variable if one does not already exist, and return its value.

tcl.label: dict
tcl.documentation: Performs one of several operations on dictionary values or variables containing dictionary values

tcl.label: else
tcl.documentation: else statement

tcl.label: eval
tcl.documentation: Eval takes one or more arguments, which together comprise a Tcl script containing one or more commands. It concatenates all its arguments in the same fashion as the concat command, passes the concatenated string to the Tcl interpreter recursively, and returns the result of that evaluation (or any error generated by it). Note that the list command quotes sequences of words in such a way that they are not further expanded by the eval command.

tcl.label: expr
tcl.documentation: Concatenates args (adding separator spaces between them), evaluates the result as a Tcl expression, and returns the value. The operators permitted in Tcl expressions include a subset of the operators permitted in C expressions. For those operators common to both Tcl and C, Tcl applies the same meaning and precedence as the corresponding C operators. Expressions almost always yield numeric results (integer or floating-point values).

tcl.label: file
tcl.documentation: This command provides several operations on a file's name or attributes. Name is the name of a file; if it starts with a tilde, then tilde substitution is done before executing the command. Option indicates what to do with the file name. Any unique abbreviation for option is acceptable.

tcl.label: glob
tcl.detail: Return names of files that match patterns
tcl.documentation: This command performs file name “globbing” in a fashion similar to the csh shell or bash shell. It returns a list of the files whose names match any of the pattern arguments. No particular order is guaranteed in the list, so if a sorted list is required the caller should use lsort.

tcl.label: iffe
tcl.detail: Execute scripts conditionally - if elseif else
tcl.documentation: if elseif else statement

tcl.label: incr
tcl.detail: Increment the value of a variable
tcl.documentation: Increments the value stored in the variable whose name is varName. The value of the variable must be an integer. If increment is supplied then its value (which must be an integer) is added to the value of variable varName; otherwise 1 is added to varName. The new value is stored as a decimal string in variable varName and also returned as result. Starting with the Tcl 8.5 release, the variable varName passed to incr may be unset, and in that case, it will be set to the value increment or to the default increment value of 1.

tcl.label: join
tcl.detail: Create a string by joining together list of elements
tcl.documentation: The list argument must be a valid Tcl list. This command returns the string formed by joining all of the elements of list together with joinString separating each adjacent pair of elements. The joinString argument defaults to a space character.

tcl.label: list
tcl.documentation: This command returns a list comprised of all the args, or an empty string if no args are specified. Braces and backslashes get added as necessary, so that the lindex command may be used on the result to re-extract the original arguments, and also so that eval may be used to execute the resulting list, with arg1 comprising the command's name and the other args comprising its arguments.

tcl.label: list
tcl.detail: Return one or more adjacent elements from a list
tcl.documentation: This command will return a new list consisting of elements first through last, inclusive. The index values first and last are interpreted the same as index values for the command string index, supporting simple index arithmetic and indices relative to the end of the list.

tcl.label: lmap
tcl.detail: Iterate over all elements in one or more lists and collect results
tcl.documentation: The lmap command implements a loop where the loop variable(s) take on values from one or more lists, and the loop returns a list of results collected from each iteration. 'varName' is the loop variable, and 'list' is a list of values to assign to varname and the 'body' argument is a Tcl script. For each element of list (in order from first to last), lmap assigns the contents of the element to varname as if the lindex command had been used to extract the element, then calls the Tcl interpreter to execute body. If execution of the body completes normally then the result of the body is appended to an accumulator list. lmap returns the accumulator list.

tcl.label: lset
tcl.detail: Change an element in a list
tcl.documentation: The lset command accepts a parameter, varName, which it interprets as the name of a variable containing a Tcl list. It also accepts zero or more indices into the list. The indices may be presented either consecutively on the command line, or grouped in a Tcl list and presented as a single argument. Finally, it accepts a new value for an element of varName.

tcl.label: open
tcl.detail: Open a file-based or command pipeline channel
tcl.documentation: open a file channel

tcl.label: proc
tcl.documentation: The proc command creates a new Tcl procedure named name, replacing any existing command or procedure there may have been by that name. Whenever the new command is invoked, the contents of body will be executed by the Tcl interpreter.

tcl.label: puts
tcl.documentation: Writes the characters given by string to the channel give by channelId

tcl.label: after
tcl.detail: Executes a command after a time delay
tcl.documentation: This command is used to delay execution of the program or to execute a command in background sometime in the future.

tcl.label: apply
tcl.documentation: The command apply applies the function func to the arguments arg1 arg2 ... and returns the result.

tcl.label: array
tcl.documentation: This command performs one of several operations on the variable given by arrayName. Unless otherwise specified for individual commands, arrayName must be the name of an existing array variable. The option argument determines what action is carried out by the command.

tcl.label: catch
tcl.detail: Evaluate script and trap exceptional returns
tcl.documentation: The catch command may be used to prevent errors from aborting command interpretation. It calls the Tcl interpreter recursively to execute script, and always returns without raising an error, regardless of any errors that might occur while executing script.

tcl.label: clock
tcl.detail: Obtain and manipulate dates and times
tcl.documentation: The clock command performs several operations that obtain and manipulate values that represent times. Subcommands could be one of add, clicks, format, microseconds, milliseconds, scan, seconds.

tcl.label: close
tcl.documentation: Closes or half-closes the channel given by channelId. ChannelId must be an identifier for an open channel such as a Tcl standard channel (stdin, stdout, or stderr), the return value from an invocation of open or socket, or the result of a channel creation command provided by a Tcl extension.

tcl.label: error
tcl.documentation: Returns a TCL_ERROR code, which causes command interpretation to be unwound. Message is a string that is returned to the application to indicate what went wrong. The -errorinfo return option of an interpreter is used to accumulate a stack trace of what was in progress when an error occurred; as nested commands unwind, the Tcl interpreter adds information to the -errorinfo return option. If the info argument is present, it is used to initialize the -errorinfo return options and the first increment of unwind information will not be added by the Tcl interpreter. In other words, the command containing the error command will not appear in the stack trace; in its place will be info. Historically, this feature had been most useful in conjunction with the catch command: if a caught error cannot be handled successfully, info can be used to return a stack trace reflecting the original point of occurrence of the error

tcl.label: lsort
tcl.detail: Sort the elements of a list
tcl.documentation: This command sorts the elements of list, returning a new list in sorted order.

tcl.label: unset
tcl.documentation: This command removes one or more variables. Each name is a variable name, specified in any of the ways acceptable to the set command. If a name refers to an element of an array then that element is removed without affecting the rest of the array. If a name consists of an array name with no parenthesized index, then the entire array is deleted. The unset command returns an empty string as result. If -nocomplain is specified as the first argument, any possible errors are suppressed. The option may not be abbreviated, in order to disambiguate it from possible variable names. The option -- indicates the end of the options, and should be used if you wish to remove a variable with the same name as any of the options.

tcl.label: while
tcl.documentation: Evaluate test as an expression and if true, execute body until test evaluates to false or break is encountered.

tcl.label: append
tcl.documentation: Append all of the value arguments to the current value of variable varName. If varName does not exist, it is given a value equal to the concatenation of all the value arguments. The result of this command is the new value stored in variable varName.

tcl.label: concat
tcl.documentation: This command joins each of its arguments together with spaces after trimming leading and trailing white-space from each of them. If all of the arguments are lists, this has the same effect as concatenating them into a single list. Arguments that are empty (after trimming) are ignored entirely. It permits any number of arguments; if no args are supplied, the result is an empty string.

tcl.label: elseif
tcl.documentation: elseif statement

tcl.label: format
tcl.detail: Format a string in the style of sprintf
tcl.documentation: This command generates a formatted string in a fashion similar to the ANSI C sprintf procedure. formatString indicates how to format the result, using % conversion specifiers as in sprintf, and the additional arguments, if any, provide values to be substituted into the result. The return value from format is the formatted string.

tcl.label: lindex
tcl.detail: Retrieve an element from a list
tcl.documentation: The lindex command accepts a parameter, list, which it treats as a Tcl list. It also accepts zero or more indices into the list. The indices may be presented either consecutively on the command line, or grouped in a Tcl list and presented as a single argument. If additional index arguments are supplied, then each argument is used in turn to select an element from the previous indexing operation, allowing the script to select elements from sublists.

tcl.label: parray
tcl.documentation: Prints on standard output the names and values of all elements in the array arrayName

tcl.label: regexp
tcl.detail: Match a regular expression against a string
tcl.documentation: Determines whether the regular expression exp matches part or all of string and returns 1 if it does, 0 if it does not, unless -inline is specified

tcl.label: regsub
tcl.detail: Perform substitutions based on regular expression pattern matching
tcl.documentation: This command matches the regular expression exp against string, and either copies string to the variable whose name is given by varName or returns string if varName is not present.

tcl.label: rename
tcl.documentation: Rename the command that used to be called oldName so that it is now called newName. If newName is an empty string then oldName is deleted.

tcl.label: switch
tcl.detail: Evaluate one of several scripts, depending on a given value
tcl.documentation: Returns the value of variable varName. If value is specified, then set the value of varName to value, creating a new variable if one does not already exist, and return its value.

tcl.label: foreach
tcl.detail: Iterate over all elements in one or more lists
tcl.documentation: The foreach command implements a loop where the loop variable(s) take on values from one or more lists. In the simplest case there is one loop variable, varname, and one list, list, that is a list of values to assign to varname. The body argument is a Tcl script. For each element of list (in order from first to last), foreach assigns the contents of the element to varname as if the lindex command had been used to extract the element, then calls the Tcl interpreter to execute body.

tcl.label: lappend
tcl.detail: Append list of elements onto a variable
tcl.documentation: This command treats the variable given by varName as a list and appends each of the value arguments to that list as a separate element, with spaces between elements. If varName does not exist, it is created as a list with elements given by the value arguments.

tcl.label: lassign
tcl.documentation: This command treats the value list as a list and assigns successive elements from that list to the variables given by the varName arguments in order. If there are more variable names than list elements, the remaining variables are set to the empty string. If there are more list elements than variables, a list of unassigned elements is returned.

tcl.label: linsert
tcl.documentation: This command produces a new list from list by inserting all of the element arguments just before the index'th element of list. Each element argument will become a separate element of the new list. If index is less than or equal to zero, then the new elements are inserted at the beginning of the list. If index is greater or equal to the length of list, it is as if it was end.

tcl.label: llength
tcl.detail: Count the number of elements in a list
tcl.documentation: Treats list as a list and returns a decimal string giving the number of elements in it.

tcl.label: lrepeat
tcl.detail: Build a list by repeating elements
tcl.documentation: The lrepeat command creates a list of size 'count' * number of elements by repeating 'count' times the sequence of elements 'element ...'

tcl.label: lsearch
tcl.detail: Search a list for an element
tcl.documentation: This command searches the elements of list to see if one of them matches pattern. If so, the command returns the index of the first matching element (unless the options -all or -inline are specified.) If not, the command returns -1 or (if options -all or -inline are specified) the empty string.

tcl.label: dict get
tcl.detail: Get a value from a dictionary for a key
tcl.documentation: Given a dictionary value (first argument) and a key (second argument), this will retrieve the value for that key. Where several keys are supplied, the behaviour of the command shall be as if the result of dict get $dictVal $key was passed as the first argument to dict get with the remaining arguments as second (and possibly subsequent) arguments. If no keys are provided, dict get will return a list containing pairs of elements in a manner similar to array get. That is, the first element of each pair would be the key and the second element would be the value for that key.

tcl.label: dict set
tcl.detail: Add/update a mapping for a dict
tcl.documentation: This operation takes the name of a variable containing a dictionary value and places an updated dictionary value in that variable containing a mapping from the given key to the given value. When multiple keys are present, this operation creates or updates a chain of nested dictionaries. The updated dictionary value is returned.

tcl.label: lreplace
tcl.detail: Replace elements in a list with new elements
tcl.documentation: lreplace returns a new list formed by replacing zero or more elements of list with the element arguments. first and last are index values specifying the first and last elements of the range to replace. The index values first and last are interpreted the same as index values for the command string index, supporting simple index arithmetic and indices relative to the end of the list.

tcl.label: lreverse
tcl.detail: Reverse the order of a list
tcl.documentation: The lreverse command returns a list that has the same elements as its input list, list, except with the elements in the reverse order.

tcl.label: array get
tcl.documentation: Returns a list containing pairs of elements. The first element in each pair is the name of an element in arrayName and the second element of each pair is the value of the array element. The order of the pairs is undefined. If pattern is not specified, then all of the elements of the array are included in the result. If pattern is specified, then only those elements whose names match pattern (using the matching rules of string match) are included. If arrayName is not the name of an array variable, or if the array contains no elements, then an empty list is returned. If traces on the array modify the list of elements, the elements returned are those that exist both before and after the call to array get.

tcl.label: array set
tcl.documentation: Sets the values of one or more elements in arrayName. list must have a form like that returned by array get, consisting of an even number of elements. Each odd-numbered element in list is treated as an element name within arrayName, and the following element in list is used as a new value for that array element. If the variable arrayName does not already exist and list is empty, arrayName is created with an empty array value.

tcl.label: dict keys
tcl.documentation: Return a list of all keys in the given dictionary value. If a pattern is supplied, only those keys that match it (according to the rules of string match) will be returned. The returned keys will be in the order that they were inserted into the dictionary.

tcl.label: dict size
tcl.documentation: Return the number of key/value mappings in the given dictionary value.

tcl.label: file copy
tcl.documentation: This command makes a copy of the file or directory source under the pathname target. If target is an existing directory, then multiple source files can be specified. In this case, the commmand makes a copy inside targetDir of each source file listed. If a directory is specified as a source, then the contents of the directory will be recursively copied into targetDir. Existing files will not be overwritten unless the -force option is specified (when Tcl will also attempt to adjust permissions on the destination file or directory if that is necessary to allow the copy to proceed). -- marks the end of switches; the argument following the -- will be treated as a source even if it starts with a -.

tcl.label: file join
tcl.documentation: Takes one or more file names and combines them, using the correct path separator for the current platform. If a particular name is relative, then it will be joined to the previous file name argument. Otherwise, any earlier arguments will be discarded, and joining will proceed from the current argument.

tcl.label: file tail
tcl.documentation: Returns all of the characters in the last filesystem component of name. Any trailing directory separator in name is ignored. If name contains no separators then returns name. So, file tail a/b, file tail a/b/ and file tail b all return b.

tcl.label: file type
tcl.documentation: Returns a string giving the type of file name, which will be one of file, directory, characterSpecial, blockSpecial, fifo, link, or socket.

tcl.label: dict unset
tcl.detail: Remove a mapping from a dict
tcl.documentation: This operation (the companion to dict set) takes the name of a variable containing a dictionary value and places an updated dictionary value in that variable that does not contain a mapping for the given key. Where multiple keys are present, this describes a path through nested dictionaries to the mapping to remove. At least one key must be specified, but the last key on the key-path need not exist. All other components on the path must exist. The updated dictionary value is returned.

tcl.label: file mkdir
tcl.documentation: Creates each directory specified. For each pathname dir specified, this command will create all non-existing parent directories as well as dir itself. If an existing directory is specified, then no action is taken and no error is returned. Trying to overwrite an existing file with a directory will result in an error. Arguments are processed in the order specified, halting at the first error, if any.

tcl.label: array names
tcl.documentation: Returns a list containing the names of all of the elements in the array that match pattern. Mode may be one of -exact, -glob, or -regexp. If specified, mode designates which matching rules to use to match pattern against the names of the elements in the array. If not specified, mode defaults to -glob. If pattern is omitted then the command returns all of the element names in the array. If there are no (matching) elements in the array, or if arrayName is not the name of an array variable, then an empty string is returned.

tcl.label: array unset
tcl.documentation: Unsets all of the elements in the array that match pattern (using the matching rules of string match). If arrayName is not the name of an array variable or there are no matching elements in the array, no error will be raised. If pattern is omitted and arrayName is an array variable, then the command unsets the entire array. The command always returns an empty string.

tcl.label: dict create
tcl.documentation: Return a new dictionary that contains each of the key/value mappings listed as arguments (keys and values alternating, with each key being followed by its associated value.)

tcl.label: dict exists
tcl.documentation: This returns a boolean value indicating whether the given key (or path of keys through a set of nested dictionaries) exists in the given dictionary value. This returns a true value exactly when dict get on that path will succeed.

tcl.label: dict remove
tcl.detail: Return a dict with key removed
tcl.documentation: Return a new dictionary that is a copy of an old one passed in as first argument except without mappings for each of the keys listed. It is legal for there to be no keys to remove, and it also legal for any of the keys to be removed to not be present in the input dictionary in the first place.

tcl.label: file delete
tcl.documentation: Removes the file or directory specified by each pathname argument. Non-empty directories will be removed only if the -force option is specified. -- marks the end of switches; the argument following the -- will be treated as a source even if it starts with a -.

tcl.label: file exists
tcl.documentation: Returns 1 if file name exists and the current user has search privileges for the directories leading to it, 0 otherwise.

tcl.label: array exists
tcl.documentation: Returns 1 if arrayName is an array variable, 0 if there is no variable by that name or if it is a scalar variable.

tcl.label: clock format
tcl.documentation: The clock format command produces times for display to a user or writing to an external medium. The command accepts times that are expressed in seconds from the epoch time of 1 January 1970, 00:00 UTC, as returned by clock seconds, clock scan, clock add, file atime or file mtime.

tcl.label: file dirname
tcl.documentation: Returns a name comprised of all of the path components in name excluding the last element. If name is a relative file name and only contains one path element, then returns “.”. If name refers to a root directory, then the root directory is returned.

tcl.label: clock seconds
tcl.documentation: Returns the current time as an integer number of seconds.

tcl.label: file attributes
tcl.documentation: This subcommand returns or sets platform-specific values associated with a file. If no option is provided, a list of the platform-specific options and their values are returned. If the name of a single option is provided, the value for the given option is returned. When an options are specified with values, they are set on the file.

tcl.label: clock microseconds
tcl.documentation: Returns the current time as an integer number of microseconds.

tcl.label: clock milliseconds
tcl.documentation: Returns the current time as an integer number of milliseconds.

tcl.label: #proc documentation
tcl.documentation: Documentation template for a TCL proc.

powershell.label: hs
powershell.documentation: Escape all text but evaluate variables. More: Get-Help about_Quoting_Rules

powershell.label: if
powershell.documentation: Run code blocks if a specified conditional test evaluates to true. More: Get-Help about_If

powershell.label: for
powershell.documentation: Creates a loop that runs commands in a command block while a specified condition evaluates to $true. A typical use of the for loop is to iterate an array of values and to operate on a subset of these values. More: Get-Help about_For

powershell.label: hsl
powershell.documentation: Escape all text literally. More: Get-Help about_Quoting_Rules

powershell.label: else
powershell.documentation: else defines what is done when all if and elseif conditions are false. More: Get-Help about_If

powershell.label: enum
powershell.documentation: An enumeration is a distinct type that consists of a set of named labels called the enumerator list. More: Get-Help about_Enum

powershell.label: forr
powershell.documentation: reversed for loop snippet

powershell.label: class
powershell.documentation: A blueprint used to create instances of objects at run time. More: Get-Help about_Classes

powershell.label: splat
powershell.documentation: Use a hashtable to capture the parameters of a function and then pass them to a function in a concise way. More: Get-Help about_Splatting

powershell.label: while
powershell.documentation: Repeatedly perform an action after verifying a condition is true first. More: Get-Help about_While

powershell.label: cmdlet
powershell.documentation: Script advanced function definition snippet. More: Get-Help about_Functions_Advanced

powershell.label: elseif
powershell.documentation: elseif provides an alternative path when an if condition is false. More: Get-Help about_If

powershell.label: region
powershell.documentation: Region block for organizing and folding of your code

powershell.label: switch
powershell.documentation: Equivalent to a series of if statements, but it is simpler. The switch statement lists each condition and an optional action. If a condition obtains, the action is performed. More: Get-Help about_Switch

powershell.label: foreach
powershell.documentation: Iterate through a collection, assigning a variable to the current item on each loop rather than using the automatic variable $PSItem. More: Get-Help about_Foreach

powershell.label: ternary
powershell.documentation: [PS 7+] Simplified version of if-else popular in other languages that works in Powershell. More: Get-Help about_If

powershell.label: do-until
powershell.documentation: Runs a statement list repeatedly until a condition is met More: Get-Help about_Do

powershell.label: do-while
powershell.documentation: Runs a statement list repeatedly as long as a condition is met. More: Get-Help about_Do

powershell.label: function
powershell.documentation: A simple function with a parameter block to specify function arguments. More: Get-Help about_Functions

powershell.label: manifest
powershell.insertText:
@{
	# If authoring a script module, the RootModule is the name of your .psm1 file
	RootModule = '${module:MyModule}.psm1'

	Author = '${author:Cool Person <coolperson@email.local>}'

	CompanyName = '${company:Contoso Inc.}'

	ModuleVersion = '${ModuleVersion:0.1}'

	# Use the New-Guid command to generate a GUID, and copy/paste into the next line
	GUID = '<pasteNewGUIDhere>'

	Copyright = '2017 ${company:Copyright Holder}'

	Description = '${Description:What does this module do?}'

	# Minimum PowerShell version supported by this module (optional, recommended)
	# PowerShellVersion = ''

	# Which PowerShell Editions does this module work with? (Core, Desktop)
	CompatiblePSEditions = @('Desktop', 'Core')

	# Which PowerShell functions are exported from your module? (eg. Get-CoolObject)
	FunctionsToExport = @('')

	# Which PowerShell aliases are exported from your module? (eg. gco)
	AliasesToExport = @('')

	# Which PowerShell variables are exported from your module? (eg. Fruits, Vegetables)
	VariablesToExport = @('')

	# PowerShell Gallery: Define your module's metadata
	PrivateData = @{
		PSData = @{
			# What keywords represent your PowerShell module? (eg. cloud, tools, framework, vendor)
			Tags = @('${tag1:cooltag1}', '${tag2:cooltag2}')

			# What software license is your code being released under? (see https://opensource.org/licenses)
			LicenseUri = ''

			# What is the URL to your project's website?
			ProjectUri = ''

			# What is the URI to a custom icon file for your project? (optional)
			IconUri = ''

			# What new features, bug fixes, or deprecated features, are part of this release?
			ReleaseNotes = @'
'@
		}
	}

	# If your module supports updatable help, what is the URI to the help archive? (optional)
	# HelpInfoURI = ''
}
powershell.documentation: Basic skeleton for a PowerShell module manifest, complete with PowerShell Gallery metadata. More: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/scripting/developer/module/how-to-write-a-powershell-module-manifest

powershell.label: hashtable
powershell.documentation: A key/value store that are very efficient for finding and retrieving data. More: Get-Help about_Hash_Tables

powershell.label: parameter
powershell.documentation: A parameter definition for a method or function. More: Get-Help about_Functions

powershell.label: try-catch
powershell.documentation: Attempt a block of code and if a terminating exception occurs, perform another block of code rather than terminate the program More: Get-Help about_Try_Catch_Finally

powershell.label: ctor-class
powershell.documentation: Set default values and validate object logic at the moment of creating the instance of the class. Constructors have the same name as the class. Constructors might have arguments, to initialize the data members of the new object. More: Get-Help about_Classes

powershell.label: here-string
powershell.documentation: Escape all text but evaluate variables. More: Get-Help about_Quoting_Rules

powershell.label: try-finally
powershell.documentation: Attempt a block of code and perform an action regardless of the outcome. Useful for cleanup or gracefully disconnecting active sessions even if there was a terminating error. More: Get-Help about_Try_Catch_Finally

powershell.label: class-method
powershell.documentation: Defines the actions that a class can perform. Methods may take parameters that provide input data. Methods can return output. Data returned by a method can be any defined data type. More: Get-Help about_Classes

powershell.label: comment-help
powershell.insertText:
<#
.SYNOPSIS
	${1:A short one-line action-based description, e.g. 'Tests if a function is valid'}
.DESCRIPTION
	${2:A longer description of the function, its purpose, common use cases, etc.}
.NOTES
	${3:Information or caveats about the function e.g. 'This function is not supported in Linux'}
.LINK
	${4:Specify a URI to a help page, this will show when Get-Help -Online is used.}
.EXAMPLE
	${5:Test-MyTestFunction -Verbose}
	${6:Explanation of the function or its result. You can include multiple examples with additional .EXAMPLE lines}
#>

${0:$TM_SELECTED_TEXT}

powershell.label: block-comment

powershell.label: class-property
powershell.documentation: Properties are variables declared at class scope. A property may be of any built-in type or an instance of another class. Classes have no restriction in the number of properties they have. More: Get-Help about_Classes

powershell.label: parameter-path
